Facts.text: The second Muslim caliph, Umar ibn al-Khattab, is injured in an assassination attempt in Medina by Lu'lu, an enslaved Persian captive; the caliph dies four days later
Ferdinand of Austria and Bohemia chosen as king of Hungary
First sitting of the Reformation Parliament in London
Great Patent granted to Plymouth Colony
English Long Parliament forms
England & France sign military & economic treaties
Treaty of Vilnius: Russia & Poland sign anti-Swedish covenant [NS]
Kara Mustafa succeeds Ahmed Kiprulu as Turkish grand vizier
Battle of Torgau, Saxony: Prussia defeats Austria
Britain & Spain sign Treaty of Paris
Spain acquires Louisiana
John Austin, a footpad turned murderer, is the last to be publicly hanged at London's Tyburn gallows
Battle at Wabash: Indians assault general St Clair, 637 soldiers killed
French troops conquer Maastricht
US troops under General Coffee annihilate the Red Stick Creek Indian village at Tallasseehatchee, Alabama
First permanent bank in British North America - the Bank of Montreal founded
Cuenca (Santa Ana de los Ríos de Cuenca), Ecuador, declares independence
The Times of India, the world's largest circulated English-language daily broadsheet newspaper, is founded as The Bombay Times and Journal of Commerce
First Opium War - 2 British frigates engage several Chinese junks
Palace of Gulhane Turkey, freedom of religion proclaimed
Johan Thorbeckes revises the Constitution of the Netherlands
A British fleet bombards Canton
Battle of Port Royal, fought in Port Royal Sound, South Carolina begins, Union victory (US Civil War)
Battle until the 5th November between gunboats at Bayou Teche, a waterway in Louisiana
Battle of Grand Coteau in Southwestern Louisiana in the American Civil War
John Willis Menard from Louisiana is elected the first black US Congressman (opposition to his election means he never sits in Congress)
Canada's Hamilton Football Club forms
James Theodore Holly, elected bishop of Haiti
American Old West: Self-described "Black Bart the poet" gets away with his last stagecoach robbery, but leaves an incriminating clue that eventually leads to his capture
Race riots kill 4 blacks in Danville Virginia
US Supreme Court decides federal courts have no jurisdiction over Native American tribal Council (Ex parte Crow Dog)
Tacoma vigilantes drive out Chinese, burn their homes & businesses
Friars of Tilburg arrives on Curacao
Concertgebouw Orchestra debuts in the Concertgebouw, Amsterdam, under the baton of founding conductor Willem Kes
Chaplain Ariëns founds first Roman Catholic Workers group
J H Hunter patents portable weighing scales
Martha Hughes Cannon of Utah elected 1st female state senator in the US
First US automobile show opens at Madison Square Garden (NYC)
Colombia grants independence to Panama
International Radiotelegraph Conference in Berlin selects "SOS" (· · · – – – · · ·) distress signal as the worldwide standard for help
First modern elastic brassiere is patented by New York socialite Caresse Crosby
Treaty establishes British suzerainty over Qatar
1st class US mail now costs 3 cents per ounce
Austro-Hungarian Empire dissolves
Poland proclaims independence from Russia after WW I
Thousands of revolutionary German sailors with the fleet at Kiel mutiny, seize the city, and set up councils of workers and sailors
Greek parliament bans Prince Andreas for life
22.3 cm rainfall at Somerset, Vermont (state record)
Tropical storm flooding kills 84 in Winooski River Valley, Vermont
First vehicular tunnel to a foreign country (Detroit-Windsor) opens
First commercially produced synthetic rubber manufactured
George II returns to Greece after 12 years and regains monarchy
Korean runner Sohn Kee-chung sets new men's marathon world record, 2:26:42 in Tokyo, Japan
Philadelphia Eagles beat Boston Redskins, 7-6 at Fenway Park; respective quarterbacks combine to throw an NFL record 11 interceptions
Frenchman Maurice Archambaud cycles world record for distance in one hour, 45.796 km in Vigorelli, Milan, Italy
Clare Booth's "Margin for Error" premieres in NYC
12th day of battle at El Alamein: Scottish assault
William L. Dawson elected to the US Congress from Chicago
P-47D Thunderbolt shot down above North Holland
Allied commandos land at Westkapelle, on the Dutch island of Walcheren
German troops in Vlissingen surrender
Pro-German government of Hungary flees
US 28th Infantry division occupies Schmidt Hurtgenwald
Dmitri Shostakovich's 9th Symphony premieres in 25th season opening concert of the Leningrad Philharmonic Orchestra, under Yevgeny Mravinsky

Kleggie Hermsen and Bones McKinney each score 17 points as Washington Capitols beat Philadelphia Warriors, 77-70 at Washington Coliseum to win first of 15 straight games to start season; best start in NBA history
Egypt protests German retribution payments to Israel
"Tokyo Story", Japanese film directed by Yasujirō Ozu, starring Chishū Ryū, Chieko Higashiyama and Setsuko Hara, is released
MLB Rules Committee restores the sacrifice fly rule, eliminated in 1939; rule says a sacrifice fly is not charged as a time at bat
Ishirō Honda's giant monster movie "Godzilla" goes into wide release in Japan; film makes a big splash, spawns three dozen related titles and sequels
Max Born and Walter Bothe win Nobel Prize for Physics
Australia takes control of Cocos Islands
First virus crystallized (announced)
Suez Crisis: After several days of fighting, Israeli forces capture the Gaza Strip
USSR performs nuclear test
Ivory Coast adopts constitution
Pittsburgh Pirates starter Vern Law (20-9, 18 complete games) wins MLB's Cy Young Award
USSR performs nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya USSR
For 1st time since 1800, residents of Washington, D.C. permitted to vote
Philadelphia voters narrowly approve $25 million to build a new multi-purpose stadium that would become Veterans Stadium, home to MLB Phillies and NFL Eagles
Vietnam War: The Battle of Dak To begins, becoming one of the bloodiest battles of the war
Ex-Prime Minister of Greece Georgios Papandreou buried. 300,000 demonstrate against fascist junta
New York Jets kicker Jim Turner lands 6 field goals and an extra point to beat Buffalo Bills, 25-21 at Shea Stadium
Congo president Mobutu visits Belgium
Future Baseball Hall of Fame pitcher Bob Gibson wins his second NL Cy Young Award (23-7 record, 274 strikeouts and 3.12 ERA)
"Play Misty For Me" premieres
Atlantic Records releases "Abandoned Luncheonette", the second studio album by the American pop rock duo Daryl Hall & John Oates
BYU receiver Jay Miller sets an NCAA football single game record with 22 catches (for 263 yards) en route to a 56-21 victory over New Mexico at Cougar Stadium, Provo, Utah
Mariner 10 launched-1st Venus pics, 1st mission to Mercury
Good Morning America premieres on ABC (David Hartman & Nancy Dussault)
U.S. advice columnist for "Good Housekeeping" Ann Landers asks parents in a mail-in survey "‘If you had it to do over again, would you have children?" 70% of participants say no
Dominica gains independence from UK & adopts constitution
First broadcast of "Different Strokes" on NBC TV
USSR & Vietnam sign peace & friendship treaty
Five people mortally wounded during anti-Ku Klux Klan demonstration in NC
Moroccan offensive against Polisario Front
After acquiring MLB's Oakland A's for $12.7m, Walter Haas Jr. appoints himself CEO; leads team to 4 AL West Divisional titles and 1989 World Series Championship
Ianford Wilson's "5th of July" premieres in NYC
Walter Hass Jr becomes CEO of Oakland A's
Milwaukee Brewers future Baseball Hall of Fame reliever Rollie Fingers (6-3 record, 28 saves and 1.04 ERA) wins American League Cy Young Award, with 22 of 28 possible 1st-place votes
San Diego guard Randy Smith plays his 845th consecutive game in Clippers' 130-111 loss in Philadelphia; passes Johnny “Red” Kerr's NBA record; goes on to play in 906 straight games
Nashville Network begins on cable TV
3,000 die in 3 day anti-Sikh riot in India
President Alfonsín's Radical Burgerunie wins in Argentine legislative elections
Federated States of Micronesia signs Compact of Free Association with US
Joaquim Chissano is elected the second President of Mozambique
Lebanese magazine Ash Shirra reveals secret US arms sales to Iran
Northern Mariana Islands becomes a Commonwealth associated with US
On Wall Street, after 5 consecutive gains, Dow Jones Industrial Average down 50.56
Pakistan claims it downed Afghan warplane
Soviet Union agrees to allow teaching of Hebrew
100s of Bulgarian demonstrate in Sofia for democratic rights
NBA expansion Minnesota Timberwolves make NBA debut; lose 106-94 against SuperSonics at Seattle Center Coliseum; Tyrone Corbin T-Wolves top scorer with 20 points

Šarūnas Marčiulionis and Alexander Volkov become first Russians to play in a regular season NBA game; Marciulionis scores 19 as Golden State lose 136-106 to Phoenix; Volkov held scoreless in Atlanta's 126-103 defeat by Indiana
Gro Harlem Brundtland is installed as Prime Minister of Norway

MLB club owners agree to pay players $280 million in damages under a settlement to close aftermath of the owners' 3-year conspiracy case against free agents; largest owner-to-player payment ever made in sports

TCU quarterback Matt Vogler completes 44 passes including 5 touchdowns for an NCAA football record 690 passing yards in a 56-35 loss to Houston at the Astrodome; Cougars quarterback David Klingler throws for 7 TDs
Carol Moseley-Braun elected first African-American woman in US Senate, representing Illinois
Dianne Feinstein is elected to the US Senate, goes on to become longest serving female there, serving until her death in 2023
Defenseman Ken Daneyko sets New Jersey Devils 'Ironman' record by playing 322nd consecutive NHL game in 2-3 loss at LA Kings
Dutch & British astronomers find spiral nebula Dwingeloo 1
Small forward Glenn Robinson signs the then most lucrative rookie contract in NBA history, a 10-year, $68.15 million deal with the Milwaukee Bucks
Space shuttle STS-66 (Atlantis 13), launches
Susan Smith who claimed her two children were carjacked arrested for murder
Total solar eclipse in South America (4m23s)
First NBA game at FleetCenter in Boston; Celtics lose to Milwaukee Bucks, 101-100
First NBA game at Rose Garden in Portland; Trail Blazers lose 92-80 to Vancouver Grizzlies
First NBA game at SkyDome in Toronto; Raptors beat New Jersey Nets, 94-79
With a 105-91 victory over Charlotte at the United Centre, Chicago wins their first of an NBA record 72 games in one season and their first of an NBA record 37 home games won at the start of a season
Roman EmperorConstantius IIdies at Mopsucrene on route to put down his general and cousin Julian, whose troops had proclaimed him Augustus
Enforcement of expulsion of Jews ordered by KingCharles VI of Francebegins
Peace of Étaples signed betweenHenry VIIof England andCharles VIIIof France, ending an English invasion of France and French support for the Yorkist Pretender Perkin Warbeck
Christopher Columbusfirst sights land on his second voyage - the island of Dominica in the Windward Islands
English parliament passes the Act of Supremacy makingHenry VIIIand all subsequent monarchs the Head of the Church of England
Francis Drakearrives at Ternate in the East Indies and makes a trade treaty with the Sultan of Ternate, buys a cargo of cloves
Jahangir ascends to the throne as Mughal Emperor of India at Agra, a week after the death of his fatherAkbar
PrinceFrederick Henryfestival in The Hague

Treaty signed ending the Polish civil war between the Tarnogrów confederates and Saxon forces led by Augustus II. TsarPeter the Great's influence and support for Augustus II leads to the Silent Sejm and Russian involvement.
George Frideric Handelundergoes (failed) eye operation
George Washingtonorders Continental Army disbanded
French playwright, journalist and feministOlympe de Gougesis guillotined
John Adamselected as 2nd president of the United States of America
French Napoleonic armies defeated at Vyazma by a Russian force commanded by General Miloradovich
Poet and criticMatthew Arnoldbegins his series of lectures "On Translating Home" as Oxford Professor of Poetry at Oxford University
Battle of Mentana: French and Papal State troops defeat Italian volunteers led byGiuseppe Garibaldi
Ulysses S. Grant(R) wins US presidential election over Horatio Seymour (D)
Menelik IIis crowned Emperor of Ethiopia at the Church of Mary on Mount Entoto
William McKinley(R) defeatsWilliam Jennings Bryan(D) to be President of the United States
World heavyweight boxing championJames J. Jeffriesretains title; beats Irishman Tom Sharkey on points in 25 rounds at Coney Island Athletic Club, Brooklyn, NYC
Giovanni Giolittibecomes Prime Minister of Italy; a progressive liberal, he will hold his post through most of the next decade and introduce social, agrarian and labor reforms
TsarNicholas IIof Russia signs a document of amnesty for the political prisoners
William Howard Taft(R) elected 27th U.S. President overWilliam Jennings Bryan(D)
Chevrolet officially entersthe automobile market in competition with the Ford Model T
PlaywrightEugene O'Neillmakes his New York debut with "Bound East for Cardiff" with the Provincetown Players
Legendary baseball figureTy Cobbresigns as Detroit Tigers manager; replaced by George Moriarty, first man to hold MLB's 4 principal jobs: player, umpire, scout and manager
Bank of Italy renamedBank of America
Getúlio Vargasbecame Head of the Provisional Government in Brazil after a bloodless coup on October 24.
NY Yankees 1st basemanLou Gehrigwins American League Triple Crown after hitting .363 with 49 HRs and 165 RBI; but Detroit Tigers catcher-managerMickey Cochrane(.320, 2 HRs, 76 RBI) is named AL MVP
PresidentFranklin D. Rooseveltwins a second term in office, defeating Republican candidate Alf Landon in the most lopsided election in American history in terms of electoral vote
Boston Red Sox outfielderTed Williamswins American League Triple Crown (.356 average, 36 HRs, 137 RBI); but NY Yankees pitcher Joe Gordon is AL MVP
Dmitri Shostakovich's 8th Symphony premieres in Moscow
Emperor Hirohitoproclaims a new Japanese constitution
Branch Rickeysigns 5-year contract as Vice-President and General Manager of MLB Pittsburgh Pirates
Clarence Birdseyemarkets frozen peas
Argentine ex-presidentJuan Perónarrives in Nicaragua
David Ben-Gurion's Mapai party wins the largest number of seats in Israeli's parliamentary election
UN General Assembly unanimously electsU Thantas acting Secretary-General after the death ofDag Hammarskjöldin a plane crash
SF Warriors centerWilt Chamberlainscores 72 points in 127-115 defeat to LA Lakers at LA Memorial Sports Arena; then 4th-highest point total in NBA history; remains 6th highest game total
Lyndon B. Johnsonis elected President of the United States in a landslide, withHubert Humphreyas his vice president, defeating Republican candidate Barry Goldwater
Los Angeles Dodgers pitcherSandy Koufaxis named Cy Young Award winner by a unanimous vote and for a third time; posts 26-8 record, 1.73 ERA and record-shattering 382 strikeouts
Bob Packwoodis elected senator of Oregon

David Pearsonis declared NASCAR Grand National champion asCale Yarboroughwins season ending Peach State 200 at Jefferson, Georgia; Pearson finishes 126 points ahead of Bobby Isaac to secure his 2nd title

English Lotus driverGraham Hillwins his 2nd Formula 1 World Drivers Championship by taking out the Mexican Grand Prix at Autódromo Hermanos Rodríguez; wins title by 12 points from ScotsmanJackie Stewart
MarxistSalvador Allendeinaugurated as President of Chile
US PresidentRichard Nixonpromises gradual troop removal of Vietnam
Chris Evertbecomes first tennis player to achieve the No. 1 ranking as the WTA Tour rankings debut; Evert holds top-spot for first 26 weeks
Pitcher Pete Vuckovich becomes Milwaukee Brewers' second consecutive American League Cy Young Award winner; edgesJim Palmerof the Baltimore Orioles; Vuckovich, 18-6 with 105 strikeouts and 3.34 ERA
Rev.Jesse Jacksonlaunches his 1st campaign for US presidential candidacy for the Democratic party
Body of assassinated Indian Prime MinisterIndira Gandhicremated
Bill Elliottwins Atlanta Journal 500 at Atlanta Raceway to become first driver to win 11 super-speedway races in 1 season; also first driver to win $2m in prize money in a single season
French McLaren driverAlain Prostwins his first Formula 1 World Drivers Championship, finishing 4th in the Australian Grand Prix in Adelaide; wins title by 20 points from Italian Michele Alboreto
John Lennon's "Menlove Avenue" album released posthumously
Gordon Gouldissued US patent US4704583 for the laser, ending his 30-year battle to be credited as the inventor of the laser
New York Rangers' centerMarcel Dionnebecomes just the 2nd NHL player to register 1,700 career points, scoring a goal in 5-3 loss at Calgary
Oakland A's first basemanMark McGwirewins American League Rookie of the Year with 49 home runs, 118 RBI; second to win AL award unanimously; first Carlton Fisk 1972
American talk-show hostGeraldo Rivera's nose is broken as Roy Innis brawls with skinheads at TV taping
President Reagansigns credit-card disclosure-bill
Lou Piniella is named manager of the Cincinnati Reds, replacingPete Rosewho is banned for life for gambling on MLB games
Atlanta Hawks' centerMoses Malonesets an NBA record for free throws made in a career by hitting 7-of-9 in a 121-120 win over Indiana Pacers at the Omni; passes Oscar Robinson's record (7,694)
Ayrton Sennawins Australian Grand Prix in Adelaide; shortest F1 race ever run (14 laps) because of wet conditions; Senna retains his 3rd World Drivers Championship by 24 points fromNigel Mansell
"I Will Always Love You" (Dolly Partoncover) single is released byWhitney Houston(Billboard Song of the Year 1993)
DemocratBill Clintonis elected President of the United States, defeating incumbent PresidentGeorge H. W. Bush
Rock bandRage Against the Machinereleases their debut album "Rage Against the Machine" featuring single "Killing in the Name"

Greg Madduxbecomes first pitcher since Sandy Koufax to win the National League's Cy Young Award in successive MLB seasons and the first in successive seasons with different teams; Cubs 1992, Braves 1993
TV sitcom "The Nanny" starringFran Drescherdebuts on CBS in the US
First PlayStationconsole released by Sony Interactive Entertainment in Japan
Los Angeles Lakers superstarKobe Bryantbecomes the then youngest player to make his NBA debut (18 years, 2 months, 11 days) in 91-85 win over the Minnesota Timberwolves at the Great Western Forum
Japanese Admiral Osami Nagano presents a complete plan for the attack on Pearl Harbor to Emperor Hirohito
Scientists Carlton E. Schwerdt and Fred L. Schaffer announce they have crystallized the pure polio virus, meaning they can better determine the virus' chemical and biological properties
1939 film "The Wizard of Oz" televised for 1st time - hosted by Bert Lahr andJudy Garland's 10-year old daughter Liza Minnelli, as the finale of the Ford Star Jubilee series (CBS-TV)
Soviet Union launches Sputnik 2 with space dog Laika aboard, a mostly Siberian Husky, the first animal in space
Lucan, Roman poet (Bellum Civile), born in Cordova, Spain (d. 65)
Annibale Carracci, Italian painter, born in Bologna, Papal States (now Italy) (d. 1609)
Samuel Scheidt, German baroque composer, born in Halle, Archbishopric of Magdeburg, Holy Roman Empire  (d. 1654)
Osman II, Sultan of the Ottoman Empire (1618-22), born in Topkapi Palace, Constantinople, Ottoman Empire (d. 1622)
Henry Ireton, English general and parliament leader (Marston Moor), born in Attenborough, Nottinghamshire, England (d. 1651)
Willem Kalf, Dutch painter (still lifes), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1693)
Renatus Rapinus [René Rapin], French Jesuit theologian and poet (Hortorum libri IV), born in Tours, France (d. 1687)
Georg Reutter, Austrian composer and organist, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1738)
Sawai Jai Singh II, Kachwaha Rajput ruler of the Kingdom of Amber (1699-1743), born in Amber (d. 1743)
Jan Josef Ignác Brentner, Bohemian composer, born in Dobřany, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1742)
Christiaan Brunings, Dutch hydraulic engineer, born in Mannheim-Neckarau, Germany (d. 1805)
Daniel Rutherford, Scottish chemist and physician (isolation of nitrogen in 1772), born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1819)
August Gottlieb Meißner, German Enlightenment author who helped develop the detective story genre in German literature, born in Bautzen, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1807)
Friedrich Christoph Gebtewitz, German composer, born in Prieschka, Meissen, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1805)
Victor Dourlen, French composer, born in Dunkerque, France (d. 1864)
Friedrich Wollank, German composer, born in Berlin, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1831)
William Cullen Bryant, American poet (Thanatopsis), born in Cummington, Massachusetts (d. 1878)
Mason James Murray, American senator and Confederate diplomat (Trent Affair), born in Fairfax, Virginia (d. 1871)
William Sprague III, American politician (U.S. Senator from Rhode Island 1842-44)), born in Cranston, Rhode Island (d. 1856)
Karl Baedeker, German publisher (known for guidebooks for tourists), born in Essen, Germany (d. 1859)
Vincenzo Bellini, Italian opera composer (La Sonnambula, Norma), born in Catania, Sicily (d. 1835)
Adrien Louis Boieldieu, French composer, born in Paris (d. 1883)
John Mitchel, Irish nationalist (Jail Journal), born in Camnish, County Londonderry (d. 1875)
Jubal Early, American General (Confederate Army), born in Franklin County, Virginia (d. 1894)
Gustavus Adolphus DeRussy, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1891)
Jasper Adalmorn Maltby, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Kingsville, Ohio (d. 1867)
Joseph Hellmesberger, Sr, Austrian violinist, composer, conductor, and pedagogue (Vienna Conservatory, 1851-93), born in Vienna, Austrian Empire (d. 1893)
Jacob Maarten van Bemmelen, Dutch physicist and chemist, born in Almelo, Netherlands (d. 1911)
John Esten Cooke, American novelist (The Virginia Comedians) and Confederate States Army, born in Winchester, Virginia (d. 1886)
Ignatius Donnelly, American politician, writer, and amateur scientist, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1901)
Edward D. Tracy, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Macon, Georgia (d. 1863)
Johannes Eugenius Bülow Warming, Danish botanist (plant ecology, environment-organism interactions), born in Mandø, Denmark (d. 1924)
Edward Douglass White, American politician and jurist (9th Chief Justice of the United States), born in Thibodaux, Louisiana (d. 1921)
Meiji [Mutsuhito], 122nd Emperor of Japan (1867-1912) and first monarch of the Empire of Japan, born in Kyōto, Japan (d. 1912)

Jokichi Takamine, Japanese-American biochemist who isolated adrenaline (epinephrine), which causes the body to respond to emergencies, the first pure hormone isolated from natural sources, born in Takaoka, Toyama, Japan (d. 1922)
Jim McCormick, Scottish-born American baseball pitcher and manager (first Scot in MLB; NL wins leader 1880, 82; NL ERA leader 1883), born in Thornliebank, Renfrewshire (d. 1918)
Mikhail Vasilyevich Alekseyev, Imperial Russian general and WWI Chief of Staff, born in Vyazma, Smolensk Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1918) [OS]
Johann Peter Kirsch, Luxembourg church historian and archaeologist, born in Dippach, Luxembourg (d. 1941)
Billy Murphy, New Zealand boxer (world featherweight champion 1890; first world champion in any class from NZ), born in Auckland, NZ (d. 1939)
Eugène Samuel-Holeman, Belgian composer, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1942)
Harry Staley, American baseball pitcher (MLB record 9 RBI 1893; record lasts 70+ years), born in Jacksonville, Illinois (d. 1910)
Siegfried Garibaldi Kallenberg, German composer, born in Bad Schachen, Kingdom of Bavaria (d. 1944)
John Motley Morehead III, American chemist (commercial production of calcium carbide, important for welding), born in Spray, North Carolina (d. 1922)
Albert Goldthorpe, English rugby league five eighth (RL pioneer; Hunslet 713 games), born in Leeds, West Yorkshire (d. 1943)
Paul Panzer, German-American silent film actor (The Perils of Pauline, Mildred Pierce, Hotel Berlin), born in Würzburg, Bavaria, Germany (d. 1958)

Wilfred Trotter, English surgeon who was a pioneer in neurosurgery, popularized the concept of herd instinct, and was an authority in cancers of the head and neck, born in Coleford, Gloucestershire, England (d. 1939)
Emīls Dārziņš, Latvian composer (Melanholiskais valsis - Melancholic waltz), and music critic, born in Jaunpiebalga, Russian Empire (d. 1910)
Stephen Alencastre, Portuguese Roman Catholic prelate, born in Porto Santo, Madeira Islands, Portugal (d. 1940)
Carlos Ibáñez del Campo, 19th and 25th President of Chile, born in Chillán, Chile (d. 1960)
Vilhjalmur Stefansson, Canadian-American Arctic explorer and ethnologist, born in Arnes, Manitoba (d. 1961)
Edward Barrett, Irish heavyweight wrestler and all-round sportsman (Olympic gold tug-of-war 1908), born in Ballyduff, County Kerry (d. 1932)
Raffaele Casimiri, Italian musicologist and composer, born in Gualdo Tadino, Italy (d. 1943)
George Mehnert, American wrestler (Olympic gold flyweight 1904, bantamweight 1908), born in Newark, New Jersey (d. 1948)
Enny Vrede [Maria Magdalena Müller], Dutch actress and wife of Eduard Verkade, born in Utrecht, Netherlands (d. 1919)
Nat Fleischer, American boxing writer who established Ring Magazine, born in New York City (d. 1972)
Samuil Marshak, Russian-Soviet writer, translator and children's poem poet, born in Voronezh, Russian Empire (d. 1964)
William Denis Browne, British composer, born in Leamington Spa, Warwickshire, England (d. 1915)
Heinrich Campendonk, German painter and wood carver, born in North Rhine-Westphalia, Germany (d. 1957)
Eustáquio van Lieshout, Dutch priest and missionary in Brazil, born in Aarle-Rixtel, North Brabant, Netherlands (d. 1943)
Charley Barrett, American College Football Hall of Fame quarterback (Cornell undefeated National C'ship 1915), born in Bellevue, Pennsylvania (d. 1924)
Robert Lindley Murray, American tennis player (US Nat C'ship 1917-18), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1970)

Gustaf Tenggren, Swedish-American artist and chief illustrator for The Walt Disney Company during the 1930s (Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs, Fantasia, Bambi, Pinocchio), born in Västra Götaland County, Sweden (d. 1970)

Frederick Stratten Russell, English marine biologist (plankton life history and linked their depth in the seas to light availability helping explain long-term changes in the ecosystem), born in Bridport, Dorset, England (d. 1984)
Karel Salmon, Israeli composer, born in Heidelberg, Germany (d. 1974)
Gleb Wataghin, Ukrainian-Italian physicist, born in Birzula, Russian Empire (d. 1986)
Ralph Greenleaf, American pocket billiards player (20-time world champion), born in Monmouth, Illinois (d. 1950)
Adolf Dassler, German entrepreneur (founder sportswear company Adidas), born in Herzogenaurach, Germany (d. 1978)
Roger Blunt, New Zealand cricket all-rounder and broadcaster (9 Tests; BBC), born in Durham, England (d. 1966)
Leopold II, King of the Belgians (from 1934, abdicated 1951), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1983)
Charles Jones, West Indian cricket batsman (4 Tests), born in Georgetown, British Guiana (d. 1959)
Walker Evans, American photographer (Fortune Magazine), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1975)
Gideon Fagan, South African conductor and composer, born in Somerset West, Cape Province (d. 1980)
Jānis Kalniņš, Latvian-Canadian organist, composer (Ugunī; New Brunswick Rhapsody), and conductor (Latvian National Opera, 1933–1944), born in Pärnu, Russian Empire (now Estonia) (d. 2000)
Lois Mailou Jones, American painter (Peasants on Parade), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1998)
Jim Hepburn, British tap-dancer and socialist, born in London, England (d. 1995)
Joe Turner, American jazz pianist, born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1990)
James Reston, American journalist (New York Times), born in Clydebank, Dunbartonshire, Scotland (d. 1995)
Otto Bonsema, Dutch soccer striker (6 caps; Velocitas 1897, GVAV) and manager (SC Heerenveen, GVAV, BV Veendam), born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1994)
Richard Hurndall, British actor (Doctor Who, The Inheritors, It's Murder But Is It Art), born in Darlington, Durham, England (d. 1984)
Johnny Keane, American baseball manager (World Series 1964 St. Louis Cardinals; NY Yankees), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1967)
Kick Smit, Dutch soccer striker and manager (29 caps;	HFC Haarlem), born in Bloemendaal, Netherlands (d. 1974)
Vladimir Ussachevsky, Manchurian-American composer (Creation), born in Hulun, Manchuria (now Hailar, Inner Mongolia, China) (d. 1990)
Hallgrímur Helgason, Icelandic musicologist, composer, teacher and conductor, born in Eyrarbakki, Iceland (d. 1994)
Dean Riesner, American film and television screenwriter (Bill and Coo), born in New Rochelle, New York (d. 2002)
Elizabeth P. Hoisington, American Brigadier General (one of the first two women to attain the rank of brigadier general), born in Newton, Kansas (d. 2007)
Russell B. Long, American politician (Sen-D-LA, 1948-86), born in Shreveport, Louisiana (d. 2003)
Bert Freed, American character actor (Shane, Invaders From Mars), born in New York City (d. 1994)
Jesús Blasco, Spanish comic book author (Cuto), born in Barcelona (d. 1995)
Oodgeroo Noonuccal, Australian Aboriginal political activist and writer (We Are Going), born in Minjerribah, Queensland, Australia (d. 1993)
Glen Brand, American wrestler (Olympic gold middleweight 1948), born in Clarion, Iowa (d. 2008)
Tomás Ó Fiaich, Northern Irish Cardinal, Bishop of Armagh (1977-90), born in Cullyhanna, Northern Ireland (d. 1990)
Dieter Wellershoff, German author (Minotaur), born in Neuss, Germany (d. 2018)
Valdas Adamkus, Lithuanian-American bureaucrat (President of the Republic of Lithuania, 1998-2003, 2004-09), born in Kaunas, Lithuania
Harrison McCain, Canadian businessman (McCain Foods Limited), born in Florenceville, New Brunswick (d. 2004)
Zbigniew Cybulski, Polish actor (Ashes and Diamonds), born in Kniaze, Stanislawowskie, Poland (d. 1967)
George Yardley III, American Basketball Hall of Fame forward (6 × NBA All-Star 1955–60; first player to score 2,000 points in a season; Fort Wayne Pistons), born in Hollywood, California (d. 2004)
Brian Robinson, English cyclist (Tour de France: first Briton to finish 1955; first stage winner 1957), born in Mirfield, West Riding of Yorkshire, England (d. 2022)
